en.extract:
The black caracara (Daptrius ater) is a bird of prey in the family Falconidae that is found in Amazonian and French Guianan lowlands, mainly occurring along rivers. It was formerly the only species placed in the genus Daptrius but based on genetic studies the genus now includes species that were formerly placed in the genera Milvago and Phalcoboenus.
Since migration has not been observed, they are considered resident, or sedentary, remaining in the tropics year round.
The black caracara is a widespread and sociable raptor, often spotted in groups of 2–5 individuals in tall trees. Nests, built from sticks and usually containing 2–3 brown-spotted eggs, have been observed high in trees, yet little is known of their breeding habits or reproduction. Typical of caracaras, D. ater is an omnivore as well as an opportunist, known simultaneously as a predator, scavenger and a forager; together with other American raptors, particularly the Cathartidae (new world vultures) and the condors, the black caracara benefits the greater environment by consuming carrion.


Taxonomy
The black caracara was formally described in 1816 by the French ornithologist Louis Vieillot under the binomial name Daptrius ater. He specified the locality as Brazil. The specific epithet ater is from Latin meaning "black", "dark" or "dull". The black caracara was formerly the only species placed in the genus Daptrius. Molecular genetic studies found that the genus Milvago was polyphyletic with the chimango caracara sister to the genus Phalcoboenus and the yellow-headed caracara sister to the genus Daptrius. As the genetic divergence was relatively shallow, the polyphyly was resolved by expanding the genus Daptrius to include the species in the genera Milvago and Phalcoboenus.
It is locally referred to as Ger' futu busikaka in the Republic of Suriname, and juápipi {nẽjõmbʌ} by the Emberá of Panamá and Colombia. However, both of these names refer to multiple bird species within the Falconidae. German-Brazilian ornithologist Helmut Sick also referred to this species as gavião-de-anta, literally translating to "tapir-hawk".


Description
Adult black caracara are a glossy black except for the distinctive white band on the base of the tail, and yellow to orange-red feet and face. The adults appear similar to those of their closest relative, the red-throated caracara, however they have distinctly long and narrow wings and tail, as well as a black beak. Additionally, the red-throated caracara can be distinguished from the black caracara by their red throats. The average adult length is 41–47 cm (16–19 in)-. The females of this species average 350–440 g (12–16 oz) and are typically larger than the males that have an average weight of 330 g (12 oz). 
Juveniles can be identified by their dull black plumage, pale yellow face and the 3-4 black bars found on the rectrices.
When observing flight from a distance, it is notable that Daptrius ater rarely soars, but instead can be seen continuously flapping.


Distribution and habitat
It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, French Guiana, Guyana, Peru, Suriname, and Venezuela. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and heavily degraded former forest. Common habitats include gallery forest and wooded savanna that are situated between altitudes of 0 to 900 m (0 to 2,950 ft). Black caracara can also be found in mangroves and disturbed forested habitats along water.


Behavior
The black caracara is most often seen in pairs or family groups, consisting of 3–5 birds, but may also be spotted alone. They have been observed flying in straight patterns with active wing-flapping, gliding, walking along rivers, and perching in tall trees. Other common sightings have famously associated them with tapir and capybara, as they have been observed cleaning ectoparasites and insects from the animals' fur. This interaction can be considered mutualistic, as tapirs will often solicit black caracaras, by emitting a call; the tapirs then lay still on the ground to attract the caracaras for insect and tick removal. Black caracara also scavenge around human settlements, consume carrion, and exhibit the ability to catch fish along rivers and in lakes.


Food and feeding
The black caracara is known to eat almost anything, from vegetation to carrion to live prey; therefore, they are best described as an opportunistic omnivorous predator.  More specifically, their diet may consist of nestlings and fledglings of numerous other bird species, mature smaller birds (such as flycatchers, parrots, pigeons), small mammals, frogs, lizards, snakes, numerous invertebrates, small fish, palm nuts and numerous varieties of fruits. With this diverse diet, they have developed many unique foraging and hunting strategies, including directly attacking the nests of other birds, searching the canopy foliage with their beaks for insects, and showing no fear around humans, when scavenging near towns or garbage sites.
The diverse list of feeding strategies includes foraging small fish within their riverine habitats. The black caracara makes use of exposed rocks and emergent vegetation in and along rivers, walking slowly on these surfaces while peering into the water for small fish such as species of Characidae. Once the prey is spotted, it is caught using either the bill or talons then carried away from the river for consumption. Black caracara mostly fish in areas of fast moving rivers where migratory fish are forced to bottleneck and become trapped among plants or within shallow pools, reflecting their opportunistic nature.


Breeding
There is only one observational record of a black caracara nest in Brazil being built from twigs 60–70 cm (24–28 in) in length, 25 m (82 ft) high in a tree. Little else is known about their reproductive behaviors.


Vocalization
This species can also be identified from other species by their harsh, piercing, single note kraaaa calls that usually occur during flight. This call is usually repeated several times, often ending in a decrescendo. They do not exhibit diverse vocalizations, restricted to variations in length and volume of their distinctive shriek.


Conservation status
IUCN estimates a fluctuating population of 1000–10,000 individuals, with an adult population of 670–6700. Population declines have been documented over the past 10 years; however, it is not presently considered critical. The designation of least concern is attributed to their large range, ability to survive in fragmented forest, and their diverse diet.


References

The '''black caracara''' ('''''Daptrius ater''''') is a [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Falconidae]] that is found in [[Amazon basin|Amazonian]] and [[French Guiana]]n lowlands, mainly occurring along rivers. It was formerly the only species placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Daptrius]]'' but based on genetic studies the genus now includes species that were formerly placed in the genera ''Milvago'' and ''Phalcoboenus''.

Since migration has not been observed, they are considered resident, or [[sedentary]], remaining in the tropics year round.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Bildstein|first1=Keith|title=Raptor migration in the Neotropics: patterns, processes, and consequences|journal=Ornitologia Neotropical|date=2004|volume=15|issue=1|pages=84–99|url=http://globalraptors.org/grin/researchers/uploads/279/raptor_migration.bildstein_2004.pdf}}</ref>

The black caracara is a widespread and sociable raptor, often spotted in groups of 2–5 individuals in tall trees. Nests, built from sticks and usually containing 2–3 brown-spotted eggs, have been observed high in trees, yet little is known of their breeding habits or reproduction.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Whittaker|first1=Andrew|title=Nesting records of the genus ''Daptrius'' (Falconidae) from the Brazilian Amazon, with the first documented nest of the Black Caracara|journal=Ararajuba|date=7 November 1996|volume=4|issue=2|pages=107–108}}</ref> Typical of caracaras, ''D. ater'' is an [[omnivore]] as well as an [[Opportunism|opportunist]], known simultaneously as a [[Predation|predator]], [[scavenger]] and a [[forager]]; together with other American raptors, particularly the [[New World vulture|Cathartidae]] (new world vultures) and the [[Andean condor|condors]], the black caracara benefits the greater environment by consuming [[carrion]].<ref>{{cite book|last1=Byers|first1=Clive|title=Birds of Peru|date=24 March 2017|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|location=Bloomsbury|isbn=978-1-4729-3216-7|pages=46|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uiKPCwAAQBAJ&pg=PA46&dq=daptrius+ater#q=daptrius%20ater|access-date=13 October 2017}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The black caracara was [[species description|formally described]] in 1816 by the French ornithologist [[Louis Pierre Vieillot|Louis Vieillot]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Daptrius ater''. He specified the [[type locality (biology)|locality]] as Brazil.<ref>{{cite book | last=Vieillot | first=Louis Pierre | author-link=Louis Pierre Vieillot | year=1816 | title=Analyse d'une Nouvelle Ornithologie Élémentaire | publisher=Deterville/self | location=Paris | page=68 | language=French| url=https://gallica.bnf.fr/ark:/12148/bpt6k9745205x/f74.item }}<!--BHL has a scan of an 1883 reprint - same pagination http://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12830237 --></ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1979 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=1 | edition=2nd | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | location=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=391 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16109031 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''ater'' is from [[Latin]] meaning "black", "dark" or "dull".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=ater | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=ater | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=1 November 2025 }}</ref> The black caracara was formerly the only species placed in the genus ''[[Daptrius]]''. Molecular genetic studies found that the genus ''Milvago'' was [[polyphyletic]] with the [[chimango caracara]] [[sister group|sister]] to the genus ''Phalcoboenus'' and the [[yellow-headed caracara]] sister to the genus ''Daptrius''.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Fuchs | first1=J. | last2=Johnson | first2=J.A. | last3=Mindell | first3=D.P. | date=2012 | title=Molecular systematics of the caracaras and allies (Falconidae: Polyborinae) inferred from mitochondrial and nuclear sequence data | journal=Ibis | volume=154 | issue=3 | pages=520-532 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.2012.01222.x | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/256255461 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Fuchs | first1=J. | last2=Johnson | first2=J.A. | last3=Mindell | first3=D.P. | date=2015 | title=Rapid diversification of falcons (Aves: Falconidae) due to expansion of open habitats in the Late Miocene | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=82 | pages=166-182 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2014.08.010 | url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/266248448 }}</ref> As the [[genetic divergence]] was relatively shallow, the polyphyly was resolved by expanding the genus ''Daptrius'' to include the species in the genera ''Milvago'' and ''Phalcoboenus''.<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=1 November 2025 }}</ref>

It is locally referred to as ''Ger' futu busikaka''<ref>{{cite book|last1=Spaans|first1=Arie|last2=Ottema|first2=Otte|last3=Hein|first3=Jan|title=Field Guide to the Birds of Suriname|date=November 2015|publisher=BRILL|isbn=978-90-04-24929-5|pages=59}}</ref> in the [[Republic of Suriname]], and ''juápipi {nẽjõmbʌ}'' by the [[Emberá people|Emberá]] of [[Panama|Panamá]] and [[Colombia]]. However, both of these names refer to multiple bird species within the Falconidae.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Kane|first1=Stephanie|title=Bird Names and Folklore from the Emberá (Chocó) in Darién, Panamá|journal=Ethnobiology Letters|date=15 July 2014|volume=6|issue=1|pages=32–62|url=http://ojs.ethnobiology.org/index.php/ebl/article/view/226|access-date=14 October 2017|doi=10.14237/ebl.6.1.2015.226|doi-access=free|hdl=2022/22243|hdl-access=free}}</ref> German-Brazilian [[Ornithology|ornithologist]] [[Helmut Sick]] also referred to this species as ''gavião-de-anta'', literally translating to "[[tapir]]-hawk".<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Bull|first1=Wilson|title=Ungulate ectoparasite removal by Black Caracaras and Pale-winged Trumpeters in Amazonian forests|journal=The Wilson Bulletin|date=March 1996|volume=108|issue=1|pages=170–175|url=https://archive.uea.ac.uk/~e436/Peres_Wilson-Bull_1996.pdf|access-date=14 October 2017}}</ref>

== Description ==
Adult black caracara are a glossy black except for the distinctive white band on the base of the tail, and yellow to orange-red feet and face. The adults appear similar to those of their closest relative, the [[red-throated caracara]], however they have distinctly long and narrow wings and tail, as well as a black beak. Additionally, the red-throated caracara can be distinguished from the black caracara by their red throats.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Hilty|first1=Steven|title=Birds of Venezuela|date=11 November 2012|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-7136-6418-8|pages=249–250|edition=1st}}</ref> The average adult length is {{cvt|41|-|47|cm}}-. The females of this species average {{cvt|350|-|440|g}} and are typically larger than the males that have an average weight of {{cvt|330|g}}.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Bierregaard|first1=R|title=Black Caracara (''Daptrius ater'')|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/black-caracara-daptrius-ater|website=Handbook of the Bird World|publisher=Lynx Edicions|access-date=14 October 2017}}</ref> 
Juveniles can be identified by their dull black [[plumage]], pale yellow face and the 3-4 black bars found on the [[rectrices]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Bouglouan|first1=Nicole|title=Black Caracara ''Daptrius ater''|url=http://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-black-caracara.html|website=oiseaux-birds|access-date=7 October 2017}}</ref>

When observing flight from a distance, it is notable that ''Daptrius ater'' rarely soars, but instead can be seen continuously flapping.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Ascanio|first1=David|last2=Rodriguez|first2=Gustavo|last3=Restall|first3=Robin|title=Birds of Venezuela|date=23 February 2017|publisher=Christopher Helm|isbn=978-1-4081-0535-1|page=128|edition=1st}}</ref>
[[File:Black caracara (Daptrius ater) in flight.jpg|thumb|In flight, [[Pantanal]], Brazil]]

==Distribution and habitat==
It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[French Guiana]], [[Guyana]], [[Peru]], [[Suriname]], and [[Venezuela]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and heavily [[Forest degradation|degraded]] former forest. Common habitats include [[gallery forest]] and wooded [[savanna]] that are situated between altitudes of {{cvt|0|to|900|m|-1}}.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Ascanio|first1=David|last2=Rodriguez|first2=Gustavo|last3=Restall|first3=Robin|title=Birds of Venezuela|date=23 February 2017|publisher=Christopher Helm|isbn=978-1-4081-0535-1|page=128|edition=1st}}</ref> Black caracara can also be found in [[mangroves]] and disturbed forested habitats along water.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Johnson|first1=Sibylle|title=Black Caracaras|url=https://www.beautyofbirds.com/blackcaracaras.html|website=Beauty of Birds|access-date=14 October 2017}}</ref>

==Behavior==
The black caracara is most often seen in pairs or family groups, consisting of 3–5 birds, but may also be spotted alone. They have been observed flying in straight patterns with active wing-flapping, gliding, walking along rivers, and perching in tall trees.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Hilty|first1=Steven|title=Birds of Venezuela|date=11 November 2012|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-7136-6418-8|pages=249–250|edition=1st}}</ref> Other common sightings have famously associated them with [[tapir]] and [[capybara]], as they have been observed cleaning ectoparasites and insects from the animals' fur. This interaction can be considered [[mutualism (biology)|mutualistic]], as tapirs will often solicit black caracaras, by emitting a call; the tapirs then lay still on the ground to attract the caracaras for insect and [[tick]] removal. Black caracara also scavenge around human settlements, consume carrion, and exhibit the ability to catch fish along rivers and in lakes.<ref>{{cite web|last1=McCann|first1=Sean|title=Caracara interlude #1: Camp scavenger|url=http://ibycter.com/caracara-interlude-1-camp-scavenger/#comments|website=Ibycter|access-date=13 October 2017}}</ref>

===Food and feeding===
The black caracara is known to eat almost anything, from vegetation to carrion to live prey; therefore, they are best described as an opportunistic omnivorous predator.  More specifically, their diet may consist of nestlings and fledglings of numerous other bird species, mature smaller birds (such as [[Old World flycatcher|flycatchers]], [[parrot]]s, [[Columbidae|pigeons]]), small mammals, frogs, lizards, snakes, numerous invertebrates, small fish, palm nuts and numerous varieties of fruits.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Sazima|first1=Ivan|title=The jack-of-all-trades raptor: versatile foraging and wide trophic role of the Southern Caracara (Caracara plancus) in Brazil, with comments on feeding habits of the Caracarini|journal=Artigo|date=December 2007|volume=15|issue=4|pages=592–597|url=http://repositorio.unicamp.br/bitstream/REPOSIP/73576/1/WOS000208353100011.pdf|access-date=14 October 2017}}</ref> With this diverse diet, they have developed many unique foraging and hunting strategies, including directly attacking the nests of other birds, searching the canopy foliage with their beaks for insects, and showing no fear around humans, when scavenging near towns or garbage sites.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Robinson|first1=Scott|title=Habitat Selection and Foraging Ecology of Raptors in Amazonian Peru|journal=Biotropica|date=December 1994|volume=26|issue=4|pages=443–458|jstor=2389239|doi=10.2307/2389239}}</ref>

The diverse list of feeding strategies includes foraging small fish within their riverine habitats. The black caracara makes use of exposed rocks and emergent vegetation in and along rivers, walking slowly on these surfaces while peering into the water for small fish such as species of [[Characidae]]. Once the prey is spotted, it is caught using either the bill or [[talons]] then carried away from the river for consumption. Black caracara mostly fish in areas of fast moving rivers where migratory fish are forced to bottleneck and become trapped among plants or within shallow pools, reflecting their opportunistic nature.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Olmos|first1=F|title=Fishing behaviour by Black Caracaras (''Daptrius ater'') in the Amazon|journal=Biota Neotropica|date=14 April 2009|volume=9|issue=3|pages=399–401|url=http://www.biotaneotropica.org.br/v9n3/en/fullpaper?bn02309032009+en|access-date=13 October 2017|doi=10.1590/s1676-06032009000300035|doi-access=free|archive-date=19 April 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180419101726/http://www.biotaneotropica.org.br/v9n3/en/fullpaper?bn02309032009%20en|url-status=dead}}</ref>

===Breeding===
There is only one observational record of a black caracara nest in Brazil being built from twigs {{cvt|60|–|70|cm}} in length, {{cvt|25|m}} high in a tree. Little else is known about their reproductive behaviors.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Whittaker|first1=Andrew|title=Nesting records of the genus ''Daptrius'' (Falconidae) from the Brazilian Amazon, with the first documented nest of the Black Caracara|journal=Ararajuba|date=7 November 1996|volume=4|issue=2|pages=107–108}}</ref>

===Vocalization===
This species can also be identified from other species by their harsh, piercing, single note ''kraaaa'' calls that usually occur during flight. This call is usually repeated several times, often ending in a decrescendo. They do not exhibit diverse vocalizations, restricted to variations in length and volume of their distinctive shriek.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Perlo|first1=Ber Van|title=A Field Guide to the Birds of Brazil|date=11 October 2009|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-530155-7}}</ref>

==Conservation status==
[[IUCN]] estimates a fluctuating population of 1000–10,000 individuals, with an adult population of 670–6700. Population declines have been documented over the past 10 years; however, it is not presently considered critical. The designation of least concern is attributed to their large range, ability to survive in fragmented forest, and their diverse diet.<ref name=iucn/>
